content.claim: Established research from 2007–2020 documents that Roma students were overrepresented in SEN classification and segregated special-education classes in Hungary, with a threshold effect: in schools where Roma proportion is 50–75%, children are more likely to be tested and referred to segregated classes, whereas above 75% ethnic separation may occur outside the SEN classification system. Hungary's SEN integration policy has been comparatively more successful than the regional average, but this evidence is primarily historical and geographically local.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Equity and social mobility lens
limitations: Evidence base is 15+ years old; current national-scale prevalence of ethnic sorting through SEN classification is not quantified in retrieved sources
threshold-effect findings are from local qualitative studies and may not generalize nationally
a 2023 Educatio journal study attempted to quantify 2016 vs. 2021 patterns but full findings were not retrievable in search results
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
